retrieved_docs.title: Over-The-Counter Drugs Laws -- Issues of Concern -- The Combat Methamphetamine Epidemic Act (Title VII of the Patriot Act) (2005)
retrieved_docs.content: In addition, the act requires ID verification and a log book documenting the transaction, including the date and time of purchase, the amount purchased, and the buyer's name and address. The law also stipulates that the logbook does not have to be completed if the packaged product contains less than 60 mg of pseudoephedrine. Ideally, the logs would be electronic and communicated between pharmacies and states to discourage “pharmacy hopping”. [8] [9] The logbooks are to be maintained for 2 years from purchase. The law also states that no person may purchase more than 9 g of the OTC base in person or 7.5 g by mail order over a 30-day timeframe. This also restricts mail-in pharmacies from distributing more than 7.5 g to a patient in 30 days.[ USDOJ. CMEA ]

retrieved_docs.content: This process allows the FDA to identify a pattern of "pharmacy shopping" if purchasers visit multiple pharmacies to obtain as many ephedrine-containing products as is permitted. The CMEA established a strict punishment for manufacturing methamphetamines, which may result in up to 20 years in prison per offense.

retrieved_docs.content: Authorized sales by pharmacists (Statute 4215) A licensed pharmacist may sell and dispense regulated drugs to any person with a written prescription or upon receiving an oral prescription from a licensed provider The oral prescription must be promptly converted to a written script by the pharmacist and shall be signed by the pharmacist. It must bear the full name and date of birth of the patient and the full name of the person prescribing A prescription shall not be refilled unless the practitioner authorizes refilling on the original prescription When filling a Schedule II prescription, the pharmacist must sign on the face of the prescription or enter the date of filling on an electronic script A prescription for a schedule II drug shall not be filled more than 30 days after the date of issue A prescription for a schedule II drug for a future date shall not be filled more than 90 days after the date of issue

retrieved_docs.content: Unfortunately, due to addiction or financial gain, some individuals seek prescribed opioids for illicit purposes. Prescription opioids may be obtained from a friend or relative, purchased from a black market drug dealer, obtained by doctor shopping and acquiring drugs from multiple prescribers, and theft from clinics, hospitals, or pharmacies. retrieved_docs.content: 7.1.3 The immediate area in a pharmacy remodeled or newly constructed after July 31, 2011 containing dispersed, controlled drugs must be secured in a manner approved by the Office of Controlled Substances which will prevent entry by unauthorized persons. Such a manner includes, but is not limited to, the implementation of a floor to ceiling physical barrier limiting access to the pharmacy area, motion detectors, strategically placed surveillance cameras and back-up alarm systems. 7.1.4 Access to controlled substances by non-registered personnel is only permitted under the direct supervision of the pharmacist or registered practitioner or other approved licensed personnel.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2250
retrieved_docs.content: Accidental event is classified as a حادث صناعي (industrial accident) and is described as being due to a range of medication‑related incidents. Specifically, an accidental event can result from an accidental misoprostol overdose or an accidental misoprostol poisoning. It can also arise from an accidental overdose of a beta‑adrenergic blocking drug or an accidental poisoning by antiadrenergic agents. In addition, accidental events are linked to accidental poisoning by antidepressants and accidental poisoning by psychotropic agents. This collection of relationships underscores that unintended exposures to these drug classes are common causes of accidental events classified as industrial accidents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1523
retrieved_docs.content: Accidental events encompass a variety of unintended drug overdoses and poisonings, each classified as an accidental occurrence. Specific examples include accidental bulk‑forming laxative overdose, accidental misoprostol overdose, accidental misoprostol poisoning, accidental overdose of beta‑adrenergic blocking drug, accidental poisoning by antiadrenergic agents, accidental poisoning by antidepressants, and accidental poisoning by psychotropic agents. All of these incidents are described as being “due to” accidental causes and are collectively regarded as a type of “حادث صناعي,” indicating that they fall under the broader category of accidental industrial‑type events. Recognizing these diverse forms of accidental drug exposure is essential for prevention, timely identification, and appropriate management in clinical practice.

Clinical conditions associated with التسمم include drug‑induced levin‑degradation poisoning and adrenal syndrome. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 1219
retrieved_docs.content: Accidental event is classified as a حادث صناعي (industrial accident) and serves as the overarching category for a variety of specific toxic exposures. It is described as being due to accidental misoprostol overdose, accidental misoprostol poisoning, accidental overdose of a beta‑adrenergic blocking drug, accidental poisoning by antiadrenergic agents, and accidental poisoning by psychotropic agents. In addition, accidental event is identified as the cause of accidental poisoning by gases or fumes on a ship that injures an occupant of a small unpowered boat; this particular scenario is further linked by a relationship to other and unspecified water‑transport accidents that injure occupants of small unpowered boats.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1595
retrieved_docs.content: Accidental event is classified as an industrial accident (حادث صناعي) and includes several specific drug‑related incidents. It can be due to an accidental misoprostol overdose or accidental misoprostol poisoning, reflecting unintended exposure to the prostaglandin analogue. It also encompasses accidental overdose of a beta‑adrenergic blocking drug and accidental poisoning by antiadrenergic agents, indicating unintended ingestion of cardiovascular medications that reduce heart rate and blood pressure. Additionally, accidental poisoning by a psychotropic agent represents another form of accidental event. Recognizing these various drug‑related accidental events is essential for clinical assessment, prevention, and management of unintended toxic exposures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 168
retrieved_docs.content: Accidental events, defined as industrial accidents (حادث صناعي), are a subset of traumatic events and encompass unintended exposures such as accidental physical‑contact, mis‑oprostol overdose, overdose of beta‑adrenergic blocking drugs, anti‑adrenergic poisoning, and poisonings by antidepressants, psychotropic agents, and monoamine‑oxidase‑inhibitor (MAO‑I) antidepressants. The accidental physical‑contact event is linked to tooth loss (“verlies; tanden door ongeval”), illustrating a direct “due to” relationship between the accident and dental injury. These accidental mechanisms can also produce classic trauma injuries, including burns and internal chest injuries. Burn by fire is a traumatic event that generates burns (جرح حروق) and is further classified as a liquid‑burn (حرق من سائل) and a thermal burn (حرق حراري); it is mapped to the fire/burn e‑code for injury coding and epidemiologic tracking. Internal injury of the chest is another traumatic outcome that falls under the parent class “crushing injury or internal injury.” Within the broader Burns and corrosions category, جرح حروق represents the generic burn wound, with liquid burns (حرق من سائل) as a child node and location‑specific entities such as Burn of head AND/OR neck as narrower subtypes. The relationship label “associated_with” connects burns to جرح حروق, while “Due to” denotes that these burn injuries may arise from crushing injury or internal injury mechanisms. Recognizing these hierarchical and associative links—spanning accidental industrial exposures, specific pharmacologic poisonings (e.g., MAO‑I antidepressants), burn subtypes, and internal traumas—supports accurate diagnosis, standardized reporting, and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 25
retrieved_docs.content: Accidental event is classified as a “حادث صناعي” (industrial accident) and is identified as a cause of the coding category “e codes: poisoning.” A wide range of specific accidental poisonings are described as subtypes of this overarching accidental event, each linked to particular substances or drug classes. These include Accidental misoprostol overdose and Accidental misoprostol poisoning, Accidental overdose of beta‑adrenergic blocking drug, Accidental poisoning by antiadrenergic agents, Accidental poisoning by antidepressants, Accidental poisoning by monoamine oxidase inhibitors, and Accidental poisoning by psychotropic agents. The category “Accidental poisoning – analgesics” is positioned within the broader “e codes: poisoning” hierarchy and is also related to the Arabic term “التسمم العرضي” (accidental poisoning) through an inverse‑isa relationship, indicating that it represents a specific form of accidental poisoning. Specific analgesic poisonings are further detailed: Accidental

retrieved_docs.content: Poisoning by other medications and drugs is classified as a late effect of poisoning due to a drug, medicinal, or biological substance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 1182
retrieved_docs.content: Accidental events encompass a range of drug‑related overdoses and poisonings that occur unintentionally. Specific examples include accidental bulk‑forming laxative overdose, accidental misoprostol overdose, accidental misoprostol poisoning, accidental overdose of beta‑adrenergic blocking drug, accidental poisoning by antiadrenergic agents, and accidental poisoning by psychotropic agents. Each of these incidents is classified as an accidental event and is understood to result from unintended exposure or misuse of the respective substances. In the broader classification system, an accidental event is considered a type of “حادث صناعي,” indicating that such occurrences are viewed as industrial‑related accidents within medical and safety contexts. Recognizing these various forms of accidental drug exposure helps clinicians and public health professionals identify risk factors, implement preventive measures, and provide appropriate management for patients affected by these unintended toxic exposures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 232
retrieved_docs.content: Accidental events are defined as unintended drug overdoses or poisonings—including bulk‑forming laxative, misoprostol, β‑adrenergic blocker, anti‑adrenergic agent, opiate‑agonist, antipsychotic, neuroleptic, fibrinolytic and cholestyramine overdoses—and are classified under the “accidental event” node, which maps to the standardized “e codes: poisoning” category. Within the poisoning hierarchy, “e codes: poisoning” falls under the Arabic term “التسمم” (poisoning) and is a child of “تسمم بالهيروين” (heroin poisoning). Specific accidental poisonings such as opiate‑agonist, antipsychotic, neuroleptic, fibrinolytic and cholestyramine are placed under “التسمم العرضي” (accidental poisoning) and maintain inverse‑ISA links to the broader poisoning code set. Psychotropic‑related accidental poisonings are further detailed: accidental poisoning by antidepressants belongs to “poisoning by psychotropic agents”; accidental poisoning by monoamine‑oxidase inhibitors belongs to

retrieved_docs.content: intoxications and broader substance‑misuse patterns. Finally, the classification relationship “classified_as” places “تسمم بمؤثرات عقلية غير محددة” within the more specific category “تسمم بمسكّنات تعتمد على البنزوديازيبين”, reflecting clinical practice of interpreting unspecified psychoactive poisonings as benzodiazepine‑related when the offending agent is a benzodiazepine. Overall, the taxonomy integrates medication‑, biologic‑, chemical‑, and illicit‑substance poisonings, their mental‑health sequelae, and associated behavioral contexts (e.g., suicide, drug abuse) into a unified framework that supports accurate diagnosis, coding, and public‑health surveillance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 820
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Circumstances Related to Injury or Death” encompasses both “الاعتداء” (assault) and “تشويه الذات” (self‑harm). Within this framework, “تشويه الذات” is linked to “جرح” (wound), indicating that self‑harm commonly results in physical injuries. Recognizing assault and self‑harm as distinct but related subcategories under circumstances of injury or death, and understanding the direct relationship between self‑harm and wound formation, aids clinicians in accurately classifying incidents, assessing risk, and planning appropriate medical and psychosocial interventions.
